title: Silent 504 Errors — No Obvious Cause

slack.message: Customers getting 504s on checkout — backend logs look completely clean
Nothing in our logs at all, this is baffling
Could be CDN? Cache hit rates look fine though...

logs.message: 504 Gateway Timeout — upstream payment service unresponsive
Rate limit exceeded: 1000/min threshold hit, current: 1001/min

red_herrings: CDN cache hit rates are normal — not a CDN issue
Backend logs are completely clean — the backend itself is healthy
No recent deploys — not a regression
correct_root_cause: Third-party payment service (Stripe) enforces a 1,000 requests/minute rate limit. Organic traffic growth hit this threshold at 16:00. HTTP 429 responses were silently swallowed by the payment client library and converted to timeouts, causing frontend 504s. The identical 2100ms response times are the rate-limit signature — a real timeout would vary randomly.

expected_hypothesis_sequence.hypothesis: CDN edge caching issue causing origin pull storms
expected_hypothesis_sequence.rejection_evidence: CDN cache hit rate is 93-94% throughout the incident — no degradation. A CDN issue would show cache hit rate dropping toward 0%. Evidence is unambiguous.

expected_hypothesis_sequence.hypothesis: Database slow queries causing backend timeout cascade
expected_hypothesis_sequence.rejection_evidence: Database P99 latency is 42-45ms throughout the incident window. No slow query entries in DB logs. Backend logs are completely clean — a DB issue would surface errors in the backend service.

expected_hypothesis_sequence.hypothesis: Third-party payment service rate limiting at traffic threshold
expected_hypothesis_sequence.confirming_evidence: Payment service response times are identically 2100ms — not random timeouts. HTTP 429 in transaction logs starting at exactly 16:01. Rate limit threshold is 1000/min; traffic hit 1001/min at 16:00. The 2100ms is the rate-limit retry-after timeout, not a network timeout.

action_items: Upgrade payment service rate limit tier to 10,000/min (cost: $400/month, prevents $94K outages)
Add payment service 429 monitoring — current setup swallows these silently at the client library level
Implement request queuing with backpressure for payment calls
Add capacity planning alert at 80% of rate limit threshold
Document payment service rate limits in incident runbook with escalation contact
detection_gap: Payment client library converted HTTP 429 responses to generic timeouts, hiding the true error type from all monitoring. The incident was invisible for 12 minutes until frontend error rate triggered an alert. Transaction-level logs require manual inspection and are not surfaced in dashboards.
